text: Mixed nickel–iron oxyhydroxides (Ni₁₋ₓFeₓOOH) are known to be one of the most active catalysts for the oxygen evolution reaction (OER) in alkaline solution, but the reasons of their high performance and which element between nickel and iron acts as the active centre are still unclear. Using operando HERFD-XAS (High Energy Resolution Fluorescence Detection - X-ray Absorption Spectroscopy) combined with DFT+U (Density Functional Theory +U) calculations, we evidenced the local structure and electronic state of nickel and iron species in a wide composition range of mixed nickel–iron oxyhydroxides. We observed that iron is present at low concentrations (below ~25% w/w) occupies the Ni3+/4+ site of the γ-NiOOH structure as Fe3+ ions with a shortened Fe-O bond length of about 1.90 Å, probably as a consequence of the compressive forces imposed by the edge-sharing [NiO₆] structure. At higher iron concentrations, a non-active phase of γ-FeOOH is present. Density functional theory calculations revealed that the Fe³⁺ ions in the (Ni,Fe)OOH structure have near-optimal adsorption energy values for the OER intermediates, explaining the 500 times higher activity with respect to pure nickel and iron oxides. Instead, Ni sites are less active. Therefore, Fe is the active species in (Ni,Fe)OOH catalysts and its activity is optimized in the host structure that adjusts the Fe local environment to obtain the highest electrocatalytic performance.
